title: Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average
subtitle: Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.
caption:
Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average

Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.

The line chart tracks the average monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, measured in millions of square kilometers, across five decadal periods ranging from the 1980s to the 2020s, alongside a specific data line for the year 2025. The vertical axis spans from 4 million to 16 million square kilometers, while the horizontal axis marks the progression from January to December. All lines follow a consistent seasonal cycle, peaking in March and reaching a minimum in September. The 1980s line sits at the top of the distribution, showing a March maximum near 16 million square kilometers and a September minimum around 8 million square kilometers. Subsequent decades show a progressive downward shift in the curves. The 2020s line, colored in red, remains below the 1980s, 1990s, and 2000s lines for every month of the year. In March, the 2020s average sits just above 14 million square kilometers, while the September minimum drops to approximately 4.5 million square kilometers. The black line representing 2025 tracks closely with the 2020s average, dipping slightly below the decadal mean during the summer melt season before rising again in the autumn. The vertical distance between the 1980s line and the 2020s line is widest during the late summer months, indicating a larger reduction in ice extent during the seasonal low compared to the winter maximum. The data shows a consistent reduction in sea ice extent across all months when comparing the earliest decade to the most recent one.
